responses.response: The teacher shortage is real and documented; I do not dispute that. But A1 inverts the causal logic: it frames the shortage as a reason to delay structural reform, when the shortage itself is partly a symptom of the system S3 aims to fix. Hungary's selective-track system concentrates experienced teachers in gimnáziums and elite schools, leaving general schools under-staffed and demoralised. A structural shift to common schooling to age 14, if bundled with salary parity, career development, and diagnostic-support roles (as S3 specifies), would actually begin to rebalance teacher distribution and make general-school posts attractive again. The concern is legitimate: if S3 is implemented without parallel teacher-policy investment, classrooms will be chaotic and low-SES pupils will suffer most. But that is an argument for implementing S3 WITH teacher guarantees, not FOR delaying S3 while the shortage persists. Delaying structural reform to wait for the shortage to resolve independently is waiting forever—the shortage will not resolve while the structural incentives that created it remain in place. My condition stands: S3 must include enforceable teacher investment (salary, professional development, diagnostic-support roles) as a load-bearing component, not a secondary add-on. If the government cannot commit to that bundle, then yes, the reform is infeasible and should not proceed as described. But that is a failure of implementation design, not a reason to abandon the structural logic.
responses.new_condition: S3 is feasible only if paired with: (a) a legislated teacher salary floor raising general-school posts to gimn ázium parity within 3 years; (b) documented hiring targets to rebalance experienced teachers away from selective tracks; (c) diagnostic-support and professional-development roles created in general schools to absorb redeployed specialists. If these are not legislated as conditions of S3's passage, the reform should not proceed. But 'the shortage exists' is not sufficient reason to preserve the sorting mechanism.

responses.response: A8 is correct that Finland's gains came from a bundle, not from structure alone. I have never claimed that structure alone is sufficient—my argument for S3 explicitly states 'the delay bundled with teacher investment and diagnostic support.' A8's concern is that Hungary might adopt only the structural piece (common schooling to 14) and omit teacher policy and support, thus wasting the reform. That is a real risk and an honest objection. But A8 frames this as a reason to reject S3 or to copy Finland's entire bundle wholesale. I do not read it that way. The honest response is: S3 without the bundle is not S3; it is a hollow structural change that will fail. But that means the condition for S3 must be legislative bundling, not that the structural component is wrong. The bundling effect A8 cites actually strengthens my case: Finland's success came from delayed selection PLUS teacher investment PLUS support. All three are necessary. S3 as written includes all three. If Hungary's government cannot commit to all three, then Hungary should not claim S3 as its reform—it should say so explicitly, and then A8 is right: the reform will fail and damage trust. My revised condition: S3 is acceptable only if the structural delay, teacher-policy bundle, and diagnostic-support package are legislated together with entrenchment mechanisms, so that a successor government cannot strip out the support while keeping the structure. Without that, A8's fear is justified.
responses.new_condition: S3 is acceptable only if it is legislatively bundled and entrenched as a three-part package: (1) common schooling to age 14 (structural); (2) teacher salary parity and professional development for general-school roles (workforce); (3) diagnostic support and formative assessment systems in general schools (pedagogy). All three must be in the statute together, with supermajority-reversal requirements, so that a future government cannot preserve (1) while cutting (2) and (3). If any part is omitted from the legislation, the reform should not proceed.

responses.response: A4 raises a material risk: restricting state selective tracks (S2) shifts demand to church and private schools, which may not be bound by equity rules, thus worsening net segregation. This is not a hypothetical; it is documented in several countries post-selective-track caps. I acknowledged this risk in my S2 response as a condition: 'I would also require: (a) enforceable caps on non-state maintainers, not negotiated caps.' A4 is right that S2 alone, without simultaneous control of non-state intake, could backfire. But A4 frames this as a reason to avoid S2 altogether, or to accept that caps are politically unenforceable. I do not accept that conclusion. The answer is not to abandon the cap; it is to legislate simultaneous caps on non-state selective intake, backed by subsidy conditions and audit rights. Hungary's church-school sector is heavily subsidised. Subsidy conditionality is a legal tool that exists. The political difficulty is real—church maintainers will resist—but the solution is to make the subsidy condition explicit in the legislation, not to surrender to the concern that it is too hard. My revised condition: S2 is acceptable only if paired with legislated non-state-maintainer caps (same proportional shrinkage of selective seats in church and private schools as in state schools) and subsidy conditions that enforce those caps. If non-state maintainers are exempted or subject only to negotiated agreements, then S2 is indeed a shifting of selection burden to private schools, and I shift to oppose it. But enforceable closure of that loophole is achievable, and it is a condition of my support, not a reason to reject S2.
responses.new_condition: S2 is acceptable only if paired with: (a) legislated proportional caps on selective-track seats in all church-maintained and private schools receiving public subsidy, enforced via annual audit and subsidy clawback for non-compliance; (b) a definition of 'selective track' that covers all programmes labelled 'advanced,' 'gimnázium,' or 'college-prep,' regardless of institutional label; (c) enforceable closure of loopholes via charter-school or alternative-curriculum relabelling. If non-state maintainers are exempted or if enforcement relies on negotiation rather than subsidy conditionality, S2 becomes a privatisation of selection, and I oppose it.
